intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Climatarian diet significantly reducing or eliminating red meat from one's diet. The reasoning is as follows: Livestock agriculture contributes up to a quarter of global greenhouse gas emissions, with almost all of this coming from cows and sheep. In Australia, producing 1 kg of beef requires an average of 15,182 L of water. Furthermore, livestock agriculture drives 80% of deforestation worldwide, using up a third of the world s ice-free land. Adopting a Climatarian diet is a powerful way to reduce your impact on the climate. Shifting to a Climatarian diet will reduce your carbon footprint by approximately 1000 kg of CO2 every year, or the equivalent of driving from Perth to Brisbane."

Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is the ‘One step for the animals diet' eliminating chicken from our diets. The reasoning is as follows: Despite having the same capacity to suffer, farmed animals are denied the protection of animal cruelty laws that protect dogs and cats. This means that it is legal and widespread to cause immense suffering to animals raised for food.  If we can convince someone to stop eating birds, they would go from being responsible for the factory farming and slaughtering of more than two dozen land animals per year to fewer than one. Furthermore, while most cows and sheep raised in Australia spend most of their life grazing, the vast majority of chickens are raised in crowded factory farms."
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback

intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Reducetarian diet reducing the amount of meat that we eat. The reasoning is as follows: Despite having the same capacity to suffer, farmed animals are denied the protection of animal cruelty laws that protect dogs and cats. This means that it is legal and widespread to cause immense suffering to animals raised for food. This includes painful surgical mutilations (without pain relief) and in most cases, intensive and unnatural overcrowding or confinement, and often a painful and traumatic slaughter. There are over 500 million animals confined like this in factory farms in Australia today. This includes cows, pigs, sheep and chickens. Adopting a reductionist diet is appealing because not everyone is willing to follow an all-or-nothing diet. It is also a powerful way to reduce animal cruelty. For example, halving your meat consumption will spare around 50 innocent lives every year. This includes about 13 land animals, and 37 fish and shellfish every year."
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback

intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information:  "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Vegetarian diet eliminating all meat and fish from one's diet. Participants completed a 12-week compassion and caring-based training which consisted of weekly sessions á 75 min and weekly homework assignments. Participants were introduced to a way to offer love to themselves, other people, or animals. Affect dyad situations were applied where partners sit face to face, and one partner contemplates a situation to a specific theme of the main topics. The other partner listens attentively without giving any verbal or non-verbal feedback. Each session included a talk about a specific topic. The main subjects of this course were breath, handling difficult emotions, the four Buddhist Brahmaviharas, prosociality, compassion, appreciative joy, equanimity, forgiveness, gratitude, and self-compassion.

Participants completed a 12-week mindfulness-based stress reduction program that included various elements of meditation: body scan, mindful eating, emphasizing the breath, and sitting and walking meditation training. Every session contained a talk about specific topics such as different forms of meditation and problems that may arise with them. The focus of this mindfulness group was on the rather cognitive elements of mindfulness and its qualities of awareness. All sessions followed the same fixed sequence of quiet time, check-in, talk, meditation, exercise, homework, and check-out.

Participants were asked to evaluate photographic art prints that a restaurant is considering exhibiting in its dining areas: participants viewed a slideshow consisting of 15 pretested stock photographs portraying scenes of vulnerability, human connection, care-giving, and displays of emotional distress. The photos pictured female and male adults, as well as children and the elderly, and a range of ethnicities. Prior to this, in both the control and treatment conditions, with a visible timer counting down from 3 min, participants were asked to recall 10 behaviors where they acted “particularly masculine/manly” or “particularly feminine/womanly” (depending on their gender). Participants read that men and women can easily recall 12 behaviors. The task is intentionally difficult with the purpose of leading participants to infer that their performance is below purportedly acceptable standards for their respective gender, thus threatening gender identity.

Installation of of visual manipulations under the guise of a photo exhibit at the restaurant. The photographic stimuli were chosen with the following in mind: the connection between human and animal needed to be emphasized by visual overlap between the subjects, suggesting a caring, devoted, and protecting attitude.

Compassion was primed a slideshow using photographs showing people as having a caring, devoted, and protecting attitude. All participants completed an essay task that threatened gender identity and subsequently watched a slideshow. Participants then reviewed ingredients and nutritional information for the BLT, chicken, and falafel wrap presented in standard FDA tables.

Participants were exposed to a four-week behavioral intervention targeting five psychosocial variables: attitudes, perceived behavioral control, and subjective social norms regarding a low-meat diet, alongside meat attachment and eating identities (e.g., "meat-eater" or "vegetarian"). The intervention utilized four behavioral change functions: environmental restructuring (provision of meat alternatives), training (provision of recipes), education (infographics on health and environmental benefits), and social modeling (vignettes detailing successful meat-reduction stories). Consequently, the intervention comprised four distinct components: (1) free household meat substitutes for four weeks; (2) educational leaflets; (3) recipes; and (4) success stories in the form of written vignettes.
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, goal pursuit: food provision, goal pursuit: cooking skills, priming, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback

intervention_description: First, participants read a ‘decoy’ newspaper quote regarding lunch breaks to reduce the salience of the quotes about meat consumption and prevent demand characteristics. Second, they read an excerpt from a real newspaper article advocating for a specific reason why reducing meat consumption is recommended (e.g., "Meat harms your health"). All quotes referred to "scientific evidence" or a "study" without naming a specific source. Each quote was followed by an explicit statement containing a dietary recommendation taken from the website of the German Nutrition Society.

Participants were exposed to social norm message for two consecutive weeks, preceded by two weeks of no-message control. The message “More and more [retail store name] customers are choosing our veggie options” appeared in a green circle on the top right corner of the image displayed on the digital boards, covering 1/8th of the screen, and was animated with a small stretching and wobbling effect to draw customers’ attention further. The message was displayed alongside a picture of the vegetarian breakfast option during the breakfast service hours, and a vegetarian main meal during the remaining trading hours.

Participants completed an identity manipulation: Please think of yourself as a person that cares about environmental sustainability (sustainable social identity). Please write at least 4–5 sentences (25 words) about the following topic: What are things that you and other people that care about environmental sustainability do ... often? ... rarely? ... well? ... badly? While asking about both negative and positive aspects, this manipulation aimed to activate a specific identity without emotional confounds. Meal name manipulation: the vegetarian burgers were labeled as a halloumi burger and a falafel burger.

Participants completed an identity manipulation: Please think of yourself as an individual (personal identity). Please write at least 4–5 sentences (25 words) about the following topic: What are things that you personally do ... often? ... rarely? ... well? ... badly?. While asking about both negative and positive aspects, this manipulation aimed to activate a specific identity without emotional confounds. Meal name manipulation: the vegetarian burgers were labeled as a sustainable halloumi burger and a sustainable falafel burger.

Participants had an open-ended chat about meat consumption that lasted 5-10 minutes with ChatGPT-4o. The same initial prompt was entered for all participants: “I want to have a conversation about meat consumption. I currently eat meat. Are there any reasons why I should limit my meat consumption or stop eating meat entirely?”. Participants were given a list of questions they could ask ChatGPT, but were also told that they could decide where to steer the conversation. The provided questions focused on the environment (e.g., “How does meat consumption impact the environment?”), personal health (e.g., “Is it necessary to eat meat to be healthy?”), and animal welfare (e.g., “What percentage of meat in Dutch supermarkets comes from factory farms?”), and also included more generic prompts (e.g., “It would be quite difficult to reduce my meat consumption by a lot. How can I make this easier?”).

The intervention intended to educate employees on how small adjustments in meat consumption can reduce an individual’s environmental footprint as well as the risk of adverse health outcomes associated with high-meat diets. Participants joined an 18-month Meatless Monday campaign in which cafeterias promoted a discounted and dietitian approved vegetarian dish alongside the available meat options. Weekly posters delivered evidence-based health and environmental messages regarding the benefits of meat reduction.

A public commitment campaign was tested at an intervention store, to determine whether it could reduce red meat purchase frequency. Customers entering the intervention store were asked the following question: “To consider a healthy and sustainable lifestyle, would you be interested in reducing your red meat purchases by one meal per week?” If customers agreed, they were asked to demonstrate their commitment by having their name listed on the public-facing project website, www.meatofthematerstudy.squarespace.com. They were also given a small business card with the project website to see their name and learn more about the study.

Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform, with an additional tax imposed on items containing meat. The platform included nine restaurants that were based on real-world equivalents and offered a variety of popular cuisines. Each restaurant’s menu included a selection of starters, mains, desserts, and drinks (if applicable), to make the choice environment as realistic as possible. In total, participants could select from 164 unique food items for which we calculated the carbon footprint and calorie content. The menu page of a respective item displayed a red “T” icon and the text “Includes £X.XX of meat tax.” In the selection pop-up and in the checkout basket, an extra row identified the price added by the tax as “+£X.XX meat tax.” Additional rows displayed a small information icon, providing further details about the tax’s purpose and impact (“An additional meat tax has been added to this item to reflect its climate impact”) if hovered over with the mouse or touched on a mobile device.

Participants received the Educa­ted Choices Program’s (ECP) The Planet and Our Plates presentation, which describes the environmental impacts of animal agriculture versus plant-based food choices. For example, viewers learn about the land use of animal agriculture versus growing food for direct human consumption, feed-conversion ratios, the environmental consequences of aquaculture (e.g. ocean acidification), emissions from concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs), and more. Afterward, all participants received a free food voucher for a local café.  Café  staff, unaware of the video conditions, recorded whether the voucher was used for a vegetarian/vegan (veg*n) meal or a meal that contains meat.

Participants received the Educa­ted Choices Program’s (ECP) The Planet and Our Plates presentation, which describes the environmental impacts of animal agriculture versus plant-based food choices. For example, viewers learn about the land use of animal agriculture versus growing food for direct human consumption, feed-conversion ratios, the environmental consequences of aquaculture (e.g. ocean acidification), emissions from concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs), and more.  In  exchange for watching this presentation - video, participants received a free ticket to a formal university dinner, pre-selecting either an  "omnivorous" or "plant-based" meal.

Participants received the ECP’s Food for Thought presentation, which critically examines the ethical impacts of modern animal agriculture. The presentation highlights animal welfare concerns as well as the significant physical and psychological occupational hazards faced by workers, demonstrating how consumer demand drives these issues. In exchange for watching this presentation - video,  participants received a free ticket to a formal university dinner, pre-selecting either an "omnivorous" or "plant-based" meal.

The intervention was implemented in three restaurant locations of a major restaurant chain in medium-sized cities in the Netherlands. The portion sizes of vegetables on the plates of main dishes were doubled (150 g of vegetables instead of 75 g) and the portion sizes of meat were reduced by an average of 12.5%. During the intervention, restaurants were phoned weekly by the researcher to discuss the progress of the intervention.

Eight sandwich spreads were the target products, representing four pairs of a meat product and meat substitute: (1) “martino” (i.e., spicy minced meat) and vegetarian “martino”, (2) thin chicken slices with herbs versus vegetarian baloney slices with tomato and basil, (3) chicken curry salad and vegetarian curry salad, and (4) baloney slices versus vegetarian baloney slices. The sandwich department in the hypothetical retail store, established in a university lab, consisted of two refrigerators, placed back-to-back; only one was immediately visible to the participants as they entered the lab. In each condition, an equal number of products appeared in both fridges (i.e., four products each), but we provided multiple packages of the same product to make the store appear realistic. For this condition, the fridge with the sandwich spreads was not immediately visible to participants. The vegetarian spreads were placed directly next to the meat-based spreads.

The intervention was implemented in 20 stores on the week commencing January 27, 2019, for 12 weeks. The intervention consisted of moving 26 uniquely barcoded meat-free products within 4 meat-free categories (50% sausages, 30% burgers, 12% meatballs, and 8% mince) from their usual section into a newly created meat-free bay within the meat aisle. This new bay of meatfree products covered the whole bay, including top, middle, and bottom shelves. This meat free section replaced a bay where either meat was previously sold (16 stores) or where the bay previously contained chilled condiments (3 stores) or was unused (1 store)

After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce. The fish sauce serving container was changed to a bowl (with 500g of fish sauce) with a “special spoon” (that can hold 3.3g of fish sauce), instead of being served in the 750ml bottle. The special spoon, named the “less spoon”, has a hole in the middle and can extract 3.3 g of fish sauce at a time, or approximately two thirds of a teaspoon. Displayed near the bowl of fish sauce was an A4-sized information sheet introducing the “less spoon” with the short message: “Do not add more than 2/3 teaspoons of fish sauce per meal”.

After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce. The fish sauce was served in a bottle, but an A4-sized information sheet was also displayed near the fish sauce bottle. The sheet provided information regarding the amount of sodium already in noodles and the recommended sodium consumption per meal. The information sheet also showed a picture of a smartwatch with a healthy heart, a 120/80 blood pressure reading, a positive reinforcement message “GREAT JOB”, and a short message reading: “Less salty, reduces disease, weight, and bloating”.

Participants received by email a cookbook of ten easy-to-prepare and cheap meat-free recipes and a starter kit with advice and motivational tips. For a month, they received an email each week (four in total) with more advice and motivational tips. They were also asked to follow the Instagram account of the “Eat less meat” one-month challenge, where during one month, one publication was posted every day except on Sundays. Engaging stories requiring interactions were posted regularly, participants could ask their questions to the research team, and participants could communicate. The online materials targeted both reflective motivation (directly) by attempting to enhance health, environment and animal welfare concerns and automatic motivation (indirectly through capability and opportunity) by attempting to alleviate barriers to reduce meat consumption and thus increasing participants’ commitment to the challenge.

intervention_description: Two groups of households received tailored energy-saving information, including details about global warming and energy costs, plus a personalized list of relevant energy-saving measures. Both groups were asked to achieve a 5% energy reduction and received individual feedback via a website showing their percentage energy savings, the contribution of each behavior, and money saved. The first group received only individual goals and feedback, while the second group also received a collective 5% group goal and monthly feedback about the group's average and total energy savings via newsletter.

Regular supplies of soy foods were given free-of-charge to the intervention group. In both the intervention and control condition, the study dietitian made speciﬁc suggestions on how to incorporate soy foods into the daily menu, such as replacing meat with tofu in stew or fried dishes, cow milk with soyamilk, and cookies with a soya protein bar. To avoid weight gain, replacing other foods was emphasized over adding soya foods. To maintain compliance, all participants received individual dietary counselling by a registered dietitian and follow-up phone calls. They had at least six contacts with the dietitian during the ﬁrst year and were counselled on how to maintain a healthy diet according to the food guide pyramid.
intervention_mechanism: goal pursuit: food provision, info: environment, goal pursuit: planning/pledge

Cafeteria users who selected a meat dish were informed that they had the option to choose the same dish with either a small or large meat content, with all other ingredients being adjustable in size. Users were then prompted to make their meal configuration choices. Additionally, signs were placed in the serving area and at the entrance of the dining hall to explain the reduced option. This sign also highlighted with specific numbers how much more people could be nourished if agricultural land were used for bread production instead of pork production.

The dish with the reduced meat portion was set as the meat default choice. Consequently, if cafeteria users did not explicitly request the regular meat portion size, they would automatically receive the dish with the smaller meat portion. Signs were placed everywhere that cafeteria users are free to adjust their meat components.

No images were added next to the vegetarian dishes. The images showed the neutral depictions of the animals alone without any other contextual background.
